coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of PVP-passivated clove bud-derived carbon dots for antioxidant, catalysis, and cellular imaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Pandey, Anurag Kumar
Bankoti, Kamakshi
Nath, Tapan Kumar
Dhara, Santanu
coredata.dc:description: In recent times, carbon dots (CDs) are emerging for numerous interdisciplinary applications by modulating their inherent chemical functionality during or post-synthesis modification. The current study reports the hydrothermal synthesis of polyvinylpyrrolidone K-30 (PVP) passivated clove bud-derived carbon dots (PPCCDs) for multifaceted applications. The adopted technique is facile and environmentally friendly for the production of CDs with in situ PVP passivation. Physicochemical characterization of CDs is performed using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The study reveals the formation of nitrogen-doped spherical PPCCDs with an average hydrodynamic size of ∼ 4.9 nm. It is also evident that there is modulation in optical properties and quantum efficiency as a result of PVP passivation. The study further demonstrates their suitability in biological environments as observed by pH stability, photostability, and cytocompatibility results. PPCCDs have shown significant antioxidant activity against DPPH (EC50: 57 µg/mL), suppression of superoxide anion radical (EC50: 53 µg/mL), and an efficient catalytic activity towards degradation of Rhodamine-B (Rh-B) dye. UV-Visible spectroscopy unveil the reaction mechanism during antioxidant and catalytic activities of CDs that are validated by Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy with an indication of effective electron or proton donating abilities. Its bioimaging potential is evidenced through cellular fluorescence imaging with 3T3 and L929 cell lines.

Fluorescence emission spectra (a, c, and e), and normalized fluorescence emission spectra (b, d, and f) of CCDs, PCDs, and PPCCDs by varying the excitation wavelength in the range of 340–500 nm.

(a) XRD spectrum, (b) TEM image, (c) DLS size distribution histogram, (d) HRTEM fringe pattern of PPCCDs. The full scan XPS spectrum (e) and HR-XPS spectra of PPCCDs (f) C 1 s, (g) N 1 s, and (h) O 1 s.

Absorption spectra and the percentage activity of DPPH free radical (a-b) and superoxide anion (c-d), plotted against increasing concentrations of PPCCDs with their EC50 value. Inset in Fig. 4c shows magnified absorption spectra of superoxide anion reduction at 560 nm with concentrations.

(a) and (b) show the reduction of Rh-B captured by UV-Vis spectroscopy, (c) reaction kinetics plot, and (d) represent degradation plot of Rh-B dye using NaBH4 with or without adding PPCCDs. Inset in Fig. 5b shows the decolorization of Rh-B in the presence of PPCCDs as a catalyst.

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of (a) DPPH radical in absence (control) and in presence of PPCCDs at concentration of (0.0625 mg/mL) and (0.125 mg/mL) following 100 min incubation in dark at room temperature; (b) top to bottom: BMPO with Rh-B, with NaBH4, with NaBH4 and Rh-B, with NaBH4 in combination with Rh-B and PPCCDs at t ∼ 0, and with NaBH4 in combination with Rh-B and PPCCDs at t = 20 min, respectively.
Schematic of the reaction and process mechanism of DPPH radical and Rh-B dye degradation in presence of PPCCDs.

The fluorescence microscopy images represent (a) bright-field and (b-d) fluorescence imaging of 3T3 and L929 cells incubated with PPCCDs for 9 h. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent times, carbon dots (CDs) are emerging for numerous interdisciplinary applications by modulating their inherent chemical functionality during or post-synthesis modification. The current study reports the hydrothermal synthesis of polyvinylpyrrolidone K-30 (PVP) passivated clove bud-derived carbon dots (PPCCDs) for multifaceted applications. The adopted technique is facile and environmentally friendly for the production of CDs with in situ PVP passivation. Physicochemical characterization of CDs is performed using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The study reveals the formation of nitrogen-doped spherical PPCCDs with an average hydrodynamic size of ∼ 4.9 nm. It is also evident that there is modulation in optical properties and quantum efficiency as a result of PVP passivation. The study further demonstrates their suitability in biological environments as observed by pH stability, photostability, and cytocompatibility results. PPCCDs have shown significant antioxidant activity against DPPH (EC50: 57 µg/mL), suppression of superoxide anion radical (EC50: 53 µg/mL), and an efficient catalytic activity towards degradation of Rhodamine-B (Rh-B) dye. UV-Visible spectroscopy unveil the reaction mechanism during antioxidant and catalytic activities of CDs that are validated by Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy with an indication of effective electron or proton donating abilities. Its bioimaging potential is evidenced through cellular fluorescence imaging with 3T3 and L929 cell lines.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past two decades, various carbon nanostructures and allotropes containing extraordinary physicochemical properties associated with their chemical structure, distinct shapes, and sizes have been reported [1]. Notably, carbon dots (CDs) was first investigated in the year 2004 by Scrivens et al. during the process of refinement of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) [2]. Their superior properties like photoluminescence, electron donation/acceptance ability, and inhibition of photo-bleaching in comparison to traditional quantum dots or metallic nanocrystals make them the most promising in the fields of bio-labeling [3–5], free radical scavenging [3,6], catalysis [7], solar cells [8], and metal ion sensing [9,10].

There are several methods for carbonization and fabrication of CDs, such as laser ablation [11], combustion [12], pyrolysis [13], hydrothermal treatment [5,14,15], microwave irradiation [16,17], etc. The hydrothermal method provides a simple and low-cost solution for the synthesis of CDs with high reproducibility [3]. The carbon sources or precursors for the production of CDs can be classified as synthetic or natural [18], which holds a direct correlation with the functionality of synthesized CDs in terms of their applicability in various domains. Among them, natural carbon sources are advantageous because of their low-cost, minimal toxicity, and easy availability for synthesis. In the quest, novel carbon precursors that produce CDs with a high quantum efficiency, good photophysical and photochemical properties are of preferred choice.

The photophysical and photochemical properties of CDs can be modulated using two adapted techniques, viz., doping and surface passivation. These techniques involve the incorporation of dopant atoms within the core, and the attachment of various functional groups on the surface of CDs, intrinsically or extrinsically. Intrinsic passivation relies entirely on the nature of carbon precursors as well as the types of dopants present within them. On the other hand, extrinsic passivation modifies the surface of CDs using moieties not present within the precursor. Extrinsic passivation is more beneficial in terms of tailoring the functionality of CDs with different modifiers. Multifaceted applications of CDs require a careful selection of sources and surface functionalization agents without compromising their inherent properties and toxicity. In recent years, various polymers such as polyacrylamide (PAA) [13], polyethylenimine (PEI) [14,19], 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) [16], and polyethylene glycol (PEG) [17,20,21] have been reported for surface passivation of CDs.

Clove is an excellent choice as a natural carbon source to synthesize CDs. Clove buds (Syzygium aromaticum), a tropical plant belonging to the family of Myrtaceae, are grown mainly in the southern part of India. Aqueous extract of clove buds is used for the treatment of dyspepsia, gastric irritation, and has also been reported to have good antibacterial and antioxidant activities [22,23]. Therefore, the antiinflammatory and antioxidant properties favour the use of clove buds as a natural carbon source to synthesize CDs. PVP is a non-toxic, water-soluble, cytocompatible polymer that is used as a surface stabilizer in the synthesis of nanomaterials, pharmaceuticals, the food industry, and biomedicine [24,25]. Surface passivation of CDs with PVP and nitrogen as dopant can enhance their applicability in various fields. In the present context, there is no significant study on the synthesis of polyvinylpyrrolidone K-30 (PVP) passivated CDs derived from clove bud along with their multifaceted applications.

In this article, a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of PVP-passivated CDs (PPCCDs), derived from clove bud, is carried out. Unlike several other passivation techniques, the strategy employed is simple, fast, environmentally friendly, and aqueous based [14,26]. The physicochemical and optoelectronic properties of synthesized PPCCDs are analyzed and compared with non-passivated ones (CCDs). The UV-Visible and Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) spectroscopy are performed to unravel the reaction mechanism of antioxidant and catalytic potential of CDs. Furthermore, the present study also explores multifaceted applications of PPCCDs, including free radical scavenging over 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and superoxide anion assays, catalysis over the degradation of organic dye Rhodamine-B (Rh-B) in the presence of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) as a reducing agent, and the ability in cellular imaging.

The floral clove bud was purchased from the Indian Institute of Technology's local grocery store in Kharagpur, West Bengal, India. From Sigma-Aldrich, we obtained dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 3(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (98%) (MTT), butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT), nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT), and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH). Merk, India, provided sodium chloride, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), potassium dihydrogen phosphate, and disodium hydrogen phosphate. From Enzo Life Sciences, we obtained 5-tert-Butoxycarbonyl-5-methyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide(BMPO). Polyvinylpyrrolidone K-30 (PVP) and Rhodamine-B (Rh-B) were procured from Sisco Research Laboratory Pvt. Ltd. (SRL), India. The 3T3 and L929 cells were procured from NCCS, Pune, India. Deionized water was used for all synthesis reactions and analysis of CDs.

The clove buds were washed thrice with distilled water to eliminate dust particles, followed by drying overnight at 100 °C in a hot air oven. Using a mortar and pestle, the raw material was pounded into a fine powder. Then, 0.5 g of fine clove powder with and without PVP was dispersed in 30 mL of water with continuous stirring and heating at 100 °C for 30 min using a mechanical stirrer (REMI, India). The resultant homogenous reaction mixture was transferred to a 60 mL capacity autoclave chamber (TECHNISTRO, India) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h in the hot air oven. The larger particles in the supernatant were separated through centrifugation for 15 min at 10,000 rpm, followed by filtering through a syringe filter (Sartorius, India) of 0.22 µm pore size. The filtrate containing PPCCDs or CCDs (without PVP) was stored at 4 °C for further experiments. PVP-derived CDs (PCDs) was also synthesized for comparative analysis where clove powder was excluded in the premix before reaction.

CDs were analytically characterized to study their physicochemical properties that will further define their biological and non-biological applications. The X-ray diffraction characterization was performed by X′Pert Pro high-resolution X-ray diffractometer (Philips PANanalytical, Netherlands) in the range of 2θ angle 20°− 80° by using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to analyse the functional groups present in CDs by drop-casting and drying samples on KBr pellets, and measuring them with a Nicolet 6700 (Thermo Fischer Scientific Instruments, USA). UV-Visible spectrophotometer 201 (Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to analyze the absorption spectra of synthesized samples. The fluorescent property was analyzed using a spectrofluorometer RF-6000 series (Shimadzu, India). The nanostructure of CDs was analyzed employing high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100 HR-TEM, JEOL, Japan) with a 200 kV accelerating voltage. In brief, the sample was dropped on a carbon-coated copper grid with overnight drying at 25 °C to get rid of remnant moisture. The size distribution of CDs was also examined in the aqueous medium using the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument (Melvern, UK). For elemental composition profiling of CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000Verse Probe II (ULVAC-PHI Inc., Japan)) was performed as well. The Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) spectrometer was also used for validation of antioxidant and catalytic potentials of CDs, obtained on a Bruker A300 spectrometer at X-band frequency (9.6 GHz), power = 15 mW, and modulation amplitude of 5.00 G.

The antioxidant efficacy of PPCCDs was evaluated by a modified protocol for the DPPH assay [27]. The antioxidant molecules present in the reaction mixture scavenge DPPH free radicals. This changes the purple-colored stable radical cation into a yellow-colored solution and causes a loss of absorbance intensity, which can be measured via UV-Vis spectrophotometer.

In brief, 0.3 mL of methanolic solution of DPPH (0.5 mM) was added to different concentrations of PPCCDs and incubated in the dark for 100 min. The optical density post-reaction completion was measured using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 517 nm. The experiment was repeated thrice with five batches of PPCCDs to ensure reproducibility. The antioxidant efficacy against the DPPH free radical was calculated using the following Eqn 1:

The superoxide anion radical scavenging capability was investigated using the NBT assay with minor modification [28]. The reaction mixture of sodium carbonate (125 mM, pH 10.2), NBT (24 µM), EDTA (0.1 mM), and hydroxylammonium chloride (1 mM) without PPCCDs was taken as a control. The reaction was initiated by adding different concentrations of PPCCDs to the above reaction mixture and recording the optical density at 560 nm after 30 min of incubation using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The superoxide inhibition percentage was calculated using the following Eqn 2:

The catalytic activity of PPCCDs was evaluated for the degradation of Rh-B in the presence of NaBH4 using UV-Vis spectroscopy. The reactants and catalyst concentration were established by following the modified protocol [7]. Briefly, two cuvettes each of 3.5 mL capacity were occupied with 0.20 mL of newly prepared NaBH4 (0.50 M), 1 mL of Rh-B (0.01 mM), and 1.8 mL of water to make up a 3 mL complete solution. One of the above cuvettes was added with 20 μL (0.2 mg/mL) of PPCCDs, while the other served as a control for the study. The degradation of Rh-B was examined by capturing the time-dependent absorbance of the reaction mixture at a 554 nm wavelength using UV-Vis spectroscopy after 2 min time intervals. The degradation percentage (%) of Rh-B dye was calculated as per Eqn 3:

Where A0 is the initial absorbance at time t = 0, and At stands for the absorbance after reaction time t.

The toxicity of PPCCDs was evaluated using the MTT assay, wherein mitochondrial succinic dehydrogenase enzymes present in actively proliferating cells will reduce tetrazolium compound (MTT dye) into its insoluble purple color formazan crystal. In brief, 3T3 and L929 cells were seeded in 96 well plates at a cell density of 5000 cells/well in complete DMEM, high glucose media (Thermo Fischer Scientific, USA) containing Fetal bovine serum (10% FBS, Thermo Fischer Scientific, USA), and antimycotic-antibacterial (1%, Thermo Fischer Scientific, USA). A series of dilution concentrations of PPCCDs, i.e., 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, and 1.1 mg/mL were added to each well in triplicate post-48 h of incubation in an incubator maintained at 37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity. After 48 h of treatment, the MTT reagent (5 μg/mL) was added into the dark environment and incubated for 4 h more. After incubation, an equivalent amount of DMSO was added to solubilize the purple formazan crystals, and the optical density was evaluated at 570 nm using the BIO-RAD microplate reader-550. The untreated cells were taken as a control for the study and processed similarly. The cell viability was evaluated using the following Eqn 4:

Here, ASample is the absorbance of PPCCDs, ABlank is the absorbance of blank (media), and AControl is the absorbance without PPCCDs addition.

For assessing the bioimaging potential of PPCCDs, 3T3 and L929 cells were cultured on a poly-L-lysine-coated coverslip and incubated in a culture medium containing PPCCDs for 9 h. Subsequently, the cultured cells were repeatedly washed with PBS to avoid any overstaining before being captured under the inverted ﬂuorescence microscope (eclipse Ts2R-FL, Nikon, Japan). The coverslip was imaged with the help of fluorescence microscopy in bright field and fluorescence mode.

PPCCDs was successfully synthesized from clove bud using hydrothermal carbonization at 200 °C for 5 h in the presence of PVP as a passivating agent, which is depicted in Fig. S1. Under white light, non-passivated CDs (CCDs) and passivated PPCCDs are observed to be bright yellow and dark brownish-black colors, respectively. However, both CDs show green fluorescence under UV light irradiation, as shown in Fig. S2. The PPCCDs appear to be darker than the CCDs, which may be due to the passivation of CCDs with PVP.

The functional groups and bonds associated with CDs are identified using FTIR spectroscopy of CCDs, PPCCDs, and pristine PVP ( Fig. 1a). The stretching vibrations of the O-H group and N-H group exhibited a broad band in the range of 3600–3200 cm−1 [29]. Two small peaks at 2922 cm−1 and 2862 cm−1 represent symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of the -CH3 and -CH2 groups, respectively. The FTIR spectrum of PPCCDs represents a combination of signals from several bonds and functional groups present in the pristine PVP and CCDs spectra, collectively. A strong peak at 1654 cm−1 indicates the presence of CO stretching in pristine PVP. For CCDs, CC and CO stretching are present at 1620 cm−1 and 1715 cm−1, respectively. Notably, for PPCCDs, the signals from CO and CC in pristine PVP and CCDs are convoluted to form the signal at 1645 cm−1 with peak broadening. This broadening may be caused by the presence of PVP moieties on the CDs surface, as well as masking of the aforementioned functional groups. The spectra patterns of PPCCDs and pristine PVP are comparable, which explains how CDs can be wrapped with a lengthy chain of plentiful PVP [30]. Several other features in the spectrum of PPCCDs, such as an absorption peak at 1288 cm−1, 1050 cm−1, and 1027 cm−1, indicate the co-existence of both PVP and CCDs in PPCCDs. The absorption peak at 1288 cm−1 belongs to the C-N bond stretching in PVP [31]. The peak at 1050 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration bands of C-O on the PPCCDs surface, while the peak at 1027 cm−1 resembles the aromatic ether group of the added PVP [32]. The presence of functional groups on the surface of CDs makes them well dispersed in aqueous medium.

The photophysical analysis of non-passivated CDs (CCDs), PVP-derived CDs (PCDs), and passivated CDs (PPCCDs) was performed in order to understand the modulation in their optical properties as a result of passivation. Fig. 1b indicates the UV-Vis spectra of PPCCDs and CCDs with broad absorption peaks present in the range of 240–290 nm. These peaks belong to π–π * electron transition of CC bonds and their conjugates [7]. An additional shoulder peak at 324 nm in the PPCCDs represents n–π * electron transition of CO bonds [3,7]. In contrast, the UV-Vis spectrum of PCDs indicates absorption in the deep ultraviolet region, as shown in Fig. 1b. The surface passivation is responsible for an increase in absorption intensity in the wavelength range of 240–600 nm of passivated CDs, in contrast to non-passivated CDs [20]. A comparative study of the fluorescence behavior of CCDs, PCDs, and PPCCDs, as shown in Fig. 2a, 2c, and 2e, reveals a significant difference in their fluorescence emissions upon varying the excitation wavelength from 340 to 500 nm with a 20 nm increment. The passivated PPCCDs have three coexisting emission peaks positioned at 415 nm, 439 nm, and 462 nm for an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. On other hand, non-passivated CCDs and PCDs posses maximum emission peak at 432 nm and 417 nm, when excited at 360 nm and 340 nm wavelength, respectively. Specifically, the emission peaks at 432 nm for CCDs and 417 nm for PCDs are shifted to 439 nm and 415 nm for PPCCDs, respectively. A synergistic effect might be at play since an additional emission peak manifests at 462 nm for PPCCDs. Pal et al. have also reported a similar result for curcumin-derived CDs passivated with PEI [14]. The authors proposed a model for the synergistic effect on the fluorescence behavior of passivated CDs in comparison to the CDs derived from their native ones. Figs. 2b, 2d, and 2f represent the normalized fluorescence intensity for the three samples when excited within a wavelength range of 340–500 nm. PCDs have emissions at a regular interval, while the shift in the emission peaks for CCDs is irregular. Initially, the peaks in the emission spectrum of CCDs are placed close to each other with marginal differences, within the excitation wavelength range of 340–380 nm. The emission peaks shift by a relatively higher magnitude when the excitation wavelength is 380 nm and above. Interestingly, this clustering effect in the emission spectrum of CCDs is also present for PPCCDs as a result of passivation with PVP. The fluorescent characteristics of both PCDs and CCDs are present in PPCCDs. The multiple emission peaks of PPCCDs show an excitation-independent emission at and below 400 nm wavelength and an excitation-dependent emission above 400 nm excitation wavelength. These characteristics are reported in literature as well [33,34]. The uniform surface emissive trap of passivated CDs at lower wavelengths is valuable for avoiding autofluorescence issues and is also responsible for excitation-independent emission [33]. The excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior of CDs is an outcome of the various surface emissive traps with different energy levels at higher wavelengths [34]. Therefore, a possible explanation for coexisting peaks is the occurrence of different electron transitions inside the graphitic core due to different size effects and surface defects in passivated CDs [35,36]. The addition of new electronic energy states occurs when organic molecules or polymers are passivated across the surface of CDs. The emission spectrum of PPCCDs peak at 439 nm, while that of CCDs peak at 432 nm. A redshift in emission peak position is caused by this passivation of the surface. This suggests that the PVP coating had an impact on the electronic state. An electron from CD's surface quickly hops to the PVP layer, causing a hole to appear at the surface. The binding energy of excitons generated is reduced as a result of these electron-hole separations, generating a redshift. The PVP passivation prohibits the dissipation of the photoinduced carrier's from the surface sites, allowing a more efficient radiative recombination. This improves the PL property of CDs and increases quantum yield (QY). The QY of CCDs and PPCCDs is calculated and observed to be 7.6% and 10.9%, respectively (Fig. S3). Therefore, a surface passivation with PVP is necessary to improve the QY of CDs (Table S1). The QY and multifaceted aspects of various surface passivated CDs are compared in Table S2 . The photophysical properties of PPCCDs are more efficient and improved in comparison to CCDs, and their use in various applications are further studied in the next sub-sections.

The X-ray diffractrograms, TEM, and HRTEM micrographs of PPCCDs are represented in Fig. 3a, 3b, and 3d. The XRD analysis, as shown in Fig. 3a, reveals one broad peak at a 2θ value of 23.54°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [37]. The XRD peak value is shifted to 24.4°, in the case of CCDs, as shown in Fig. S4. The TEM micrograph demonstrates the spherical shape for PPCCDs in the diameter range of 1–5 nm (measured using image-J software), as shown in Fig. 3b. Nevertheless, a DLS measurement is also performed to confirm the size distribution, shown in Fig. 3c, revealing an average hydrodynamic diameter of 4.9 nm for PPCCDs with particles lying within a diameter range of 3–8 nm. This disparity in size between DLS and TEM data can be attributed to the expansion of the polymeric chain over the surface of CDs, which results in a higher diameter value in the presence of dispersion medium containing solvent than the one measured in vacuum. The TEM analysis of CCDs shows the presence of agglomeration (Fig S5a), which is not observed after passivation. The hydrodynamic diameter of the agglomerated particles for CCDs increases to 8.6 nm, as indicated by the DLS histogram in Fig. S5b. The diameter range in such a case is 6–14 nm. The HRTEM micrograph of PPCCDs shows lattice fringes with a d-spacing value of approximately 0.33 nm, as shown in Fig. 3d. The d-sapcing corresponds to the (002) plane of the graphitic carbon, which supports the conclusions drawn from XRD analysis.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to further examine the surface chemical state and composition of PPCCDs. The presence of hydrogen, carbon, and oxygen elements in the chemical structure of clove buds is dictated by the presence of polyphenolic components [38]. The scan survey, as shown in Fig. 3e, revealed the existence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) atoms at an atomic ratio of 85.30%, 10.50%, and 4.20%, respectively. High-resolution spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s as shown in Fig. 3f-h dictate the presence of various atomic bonds, validated by the FTIR results. The deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum reveals the presence of C-H, CC, C-N, C-O, and CO bonds through their respective binding energies of 283.5, 284.6 eV, 285.7 eV, 286.7 eV, and 288.3 eV [14,29]. Furthermore, the deconvoluted spectrum for N 1 s shown in Fig. 3g reveals the presence of three peaks at 398.2 eV, 399.5 eV, and 400.4 eV associated with C-N-C, pyrrolic N, and N-H bonds, respectively. This confirms the presence of nitrogen in CDs [14,37]. Fig. 3h shows the deconvoluted O 1 s spectrum with two peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.6 eV belonging to CO and C-O/N-O bonds, respectively [39].

The effect of pH and ultraviolet light irradiation on PPCCDs was performed to establish their stability under the studied conditions. As shown in Fig. S6, a variation in absorption and fluorescence spectra are observed upon changing the pH values. Interestingly, at pH 7, the fluorescence spectra show a similar intense band equivalent to the intensity at pH 11, supporting its suitable applicability for cellular imaging. Further, the impact of bleaching on PPCCDs has also been assessed post UV exposure, indicating that there is no significant change in peak intensity and position for both absorption and fluorescence spectra. Therefore, it can be concluded that PPCCDs do not aggregate and lose their intensity significantly under the studied conditions, post UV exposure.

The major problem regarding the generation of reactive oxygen species or free radicals in biological systems can be scavenged or neutralized by nanomaterials having good antioxidant activity [6,27,40–42]. The CDs can have both electron-donating and accepting capabilities, which makes them good antioxidant and prooxidant agents [43,44]. Notably, CDs are less explored in terms of their antioxidant properties, in spite of possessing minimal toxicity under in vitro conditions.[3,4,37].

The antioxidant efficacy of PPCCDs was measured by using a standard DPPH free radical assay. As shown in Fig. 4a, the absorption peaks of DPPH radicals at 517 nm decrease when the concentration of PPCCDs is increased from 15 to 500 µg/mL in the reaction mixture. Fig. 4b represents the dose-dependent scavenging ability of PPCCDs, which indicates an increase in antioxidant activity to 94% at the highest concentration of 500 µg/mL. The EC50 (concentration required to reduce 50% of the original DPPH free radical) value of PPCCDs is calculated to be 57 µg/mL. A lower EC50 value of PPCCDs signifies that it is more potent at scavenging DPPH free radicals than previously reported CDs [4,37,45]. The antioxidant efficacy of PPCCDs towards DPPH radical degradation is found to be superior in comparison to other reported CDs at their highest reported concentrations, as shown in Table S3.

EPR spectroscopy was carried out to further confirm the PPCCDs capacity to scavenge DPPH radicals, as shown in Fig. 6a. The interaction of comparable divalent nitrogen nuclei with unpaired electrons, having a g-factor value of 2.0014, results in the five-line spectra in the EPR signal. The peak strength of the signal is observed to diminish upon increasing PPCCD concentration. This occurs since hydrogen atoms attack the divalent nitrogen atom sites, which leads to the production of the stabilized diamagnetic hydrazine (DPPH-H) molecule with a reduced signal intensity [46].

The superoxide anion radical scavenging activity of PPCCDs was performed using an NBT assay. The mechanism involves the generation of superoxide anions by auto-oxidation of hydroxylammonium chloride, while maintaining a pH of 10.2 with sodium carbonate. The generated superoxide anion, in the presence of a catalyst, inhibits the reduction of NBT. The UV-Vis spectra, as shown in Fig. 4c, indicate an increase in the superoxide anion radical scavenging potential of PPCCDs through a variation in concentration from 31 to 500 µg/mL. The inset of Fig. 4c shows a decrease in absorption intensity at 560 nm with increasing concentrations of PPCCDs.

Fig. 4d represents the percentage of superoxide radical inhibition with a change in concentrations, indicating the SOD-mimicking ability of PPCCDs. The superoxide anion radical inhibition of 89% is achieved at a concentration of 500 µg/mL, with an EC50 value of 53 µg/mL calculated from Fig. 4d. This value is equivalent to or better than other previously reported literature listed in Table S3 [4,27,47].

In this study, Rh-B was designated as a modal pollutant to inspect the catalytic activity of PPCCDs using NaBH4 as a reducing agent. Rh-B is a commercially available organic dye that is most efficiently used in the textile, leather, and biological industries. It harms the environment and human health when disposed in river water without pre-treatment. Its accumulation in water results in various neurotoxic, mutagenic, and reproductive health issues in both animals and humans [48]. So, the demand for a simple and cost-effective technique using an efficient and non-toxic catalyst, is desirable for the removal of organic dyes from wastewater.

The aqueous solution of Rh-B shows a pink-red color with a characteristic maximum absorption peak at a wavelength of 554 nm. Fig. 5 represents the reduction and degradation of Rh-B using NaBH4 with and without the addition of PPCCDs as a catalyst. It can be demonstrated from Fig. 5a-b that the peak intensity of Rh-B dye decreases gradually with time after the addition of NaBH4 alone, in comparison to adding NaBH4 in the presence of PPCCDs. Hence, PPCCDs addition to the reaction mixture stimulated degradation of the dye, as observed by a significant reduction in absorbance intensity. The plot of reaction kinetics for dye degradation with and without catalysts is evaluated by monitoring the change in absorbance with time. The dye degradation kinetics are studied and observed to have a first-order linear relationship, as described by the Eqn 5:
(5)

A
A
0
= −
k

×
t
Here, A0 and A are the absorbances at times t = 0 and t, kapp = apparent rate constant, and t = reaction time.

A linear dependency of ln (A/A0) with time, as shown in Fig. 5c, confirms the first-order rate kinetics of the reaction with an apparent rate constant of 0.0887 and 0.0195 min−1 (R2 values of 0.998 and 0.98) in the presence and absence of PPCCDs catalyst, respectively. The specific rate constant is calculated by dividing the apparent rate constant kapp with the mass of the catalyst. The specific rate constant with PPCCDs is calculated to be 7.39 s−1 g−1. The specific rate constant k and apparent rate constant kapp with PPCCDs are found to be superior when compared to other reported catalysts, as shown in Table S4 [49–54]. The presence of PPCCDs as catalyst results in the faster degradation of Rh-B dye to nearly 87% (Fig. 5d) within 30 min, showing that the synthesized PPCCDs have good catalytic activity under the studied conditions.

To study the reaction mechanism of Rh-B degradation in the presence of NaBH4 and PPCCDs catalyst, EPR spectroscopy was carried out using 5-tert-Butoxycarbonyl-5-methyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (BMPO) as a spin trap at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 6b, the addition of BMPO to solutions of only NaBH4, NaBH4 with Rh-B, and NaBH4 in combination with Rh-B and PPCCDs, results in the creation of a spin adduct of BMPO-H with hyperfine coupling constants aN = 1.58 mT and aH = 2.23 mT (mostly originated from NaBH4) [55,56]. The H adduct signal for BMPO and Rh-B combinations shows negligible signal intensity. At the beginning of the reaction (t ∼ 0), the H radical adduct is made stronger by the addition of PPCCDs with Rh-B and NaBH4, indicating an increase in the reaction rate. However, after 20 min, the reaction signal drops. This implies that the formation of the BMPO-H radical adducts has decreased as a result of competition between the Rh-B and generated active species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction mechanism for antioxidant and catalytic properties
ce:section.ce:para: The electron or proton donating capability of PPCCDs was studied over free radical and catalytic reduction of Rh-B dye. The possible pathways for reduction processes of both radical and dye are based on the hydrogen donating or electron transfer capability of nanomaterials. EPR spectroscopy in Fig. 6a shows that the dominant signal for DPPH radicals can be stabilized into diamagnetic molecules (hydrazine DPPH-H) after accepting electrons or hydrogen atoms at the divalent nitrogen atom site, as shown in Fig. 7.

While Rh-B is reduced in an efficient manner, in the presence of a catalyst, through an electron transfer from active hydrogen species generated from the hydrolysis of NaBH4. The EPR spectroscopy, for studying reaction mechanisms of catalysis needs a spin adduct (BMPO) to generate a signal, since adding a diamagnetic compound with a radical produces a new radical with high stability and a strong EPR signal, as shown in Fig. 6b. The PPCCDs, due to their small size and high surface area, acts as an effective substrate for dye degradation. The process of degradation of Rh-B dye, using PPCCDs as catalysts in the presence of NaBH4, can be explained by the Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism represented in Fig. 7.

The reaction mechanism involves the oxidation of an aqueous sodium borohydride solution in presence of CDs as a catalyst to liberate hydrogen bubbles [54]. The borate ions (BH4־) adsorb over the surface of catalysts and subsequently get oxidized with water molecules to generate BO2־ ions and protonated hydrogen atoms. The role of the catalyst here is to simultaneously decrease the reduction potential of NaBH4 while increasing the reduction potential of Rh-B dye owing to their nucleophilic and electrophilic nature. The Rh-B dye and BH4־ are adsorbed over the surface of CDs, which act as a moderator (electron relay system) to receive an electron from borate ions and hand over the electron back to Rh-B through its surface [7].

The damage to the conjugated system in the Rh-B molecule, and a decrease in the potential barrier between reactant and product by a continuous generation of H2 gas bubbles during the process is explained in Fig. 7. The characteristic pink color of Rh-B changes to colorless upon reduction since the chromophore structure of Rh-B is destroyed during reduction. The reduced form of Rh-B is less toxic as compared to Rh-B. The reduction process occurs over the surface of CDs. Post reduction, the reduced dye molecules are desorbed from the PPCCDs surface and dispersed in the reaction solution. A detailed experimental and theoretical study are essential to understand the intricacies in the reaction mechanism. However, the UV-Vis and EPR analysis provide sufficient evidence that PPCCDs have electron-accepting and donating capabilities. This is speculated to be due to the presence of native phenolic components of clove, and the presence of nitrogen after PVP passivation.

Low cytotoxicity is one of the most important requirements to qualify any agent for bioimaging applications and semiconductor QDs has a major drawback in that context. Hence, to recognize the potential cytotoxicity of the PPCCDs, an MTT assay was performed using 3T3 and L929 cells. Fig. S7 shows that the cell viability of PPCCDs for both cell lines is more than 80% after 48 h of treatment, with a PPCCDs solution at a concentration of 500 µg/mL. A remarkable decrease in cell viability is observed when higher doses of PPCCDs are employed. These observations suggest that PPCCDs could be a feasible candidate for cellular imaging in biomedical applications when used at appropriate concentrations.

The bright-field and respective multicolor fluorescent images of 3T3 and L929 cells treated with PPCCDs at the different excitation wavelengths of 405 nm (blue), 488 nm (green), and 542 nm (red) are captured using a fluorescence microscope, as shown in Fig. 8 . A close investigation reveals that PPCCDs is more efficiently taken up by the cell membrane and cytoplasmic region, as evident by the strong fluorescence in those regions. The possible mechanism for the entry of PPCCDs inside the cell could be endocytosis. The untreated L929 cells (without PPCCDs labeling), as shown in Fig. S8, are found to be non-emissive when excited by laser with wavelengths of 405 nm, 488 nm, and 542 nm, respectively. The findings are consistent with previous studies, where CDs were shown to stain cell membranes and cytoplasm [37,57,58]. The multicolor imaging capability of PPCCDs is better than various previously reported CDs [3,20,59–61]. This signifies that the synthesized PPCCDs could serve as an efficient cell-imaging tool.

The hydrothermal approach is used to synthesize surface passivated clove bud-derived CDs in a single step. Physicochemical characterization reveals formation of graphitic structure of PPCCDs with nitrogen as a dopant with an average hydrodynamic size of 4.9 nm. The study demonstrated a considerable difference between passivated CDs (PPCCDs) and non-passivated CDs (CCDs) in terms of optical characteristics, size, and quantum efficiency. The PPCCDs have excitation-independent emission at a lower wavelength, which is pivotal in avoiding autofluorescence. The photostability, pH stability, and cytocompatibility tests demonstrated the suitability of PPCCDs for biological applications. Notably, PPCCDs was efficiently taken up by the 3T3 and L929 cell lines, and are mostly confined to the cell membrane and cytoplasm areas. This indicates their potential as fluorescent imaging nanoprobes. At the highest concentration of 500 µg/mL, synthesized PPCCDs show good DPPH scavenging activity (94%) and superoxide inhibition activity (89%), with lower EC50 values of 57 µg/mL and 53 µg/mL, respectively. PPCCDs have prominent catalytic activity in the degradation of the organic dye Rh-B using NaBH4. The EPR spectroscopy validates the reaction mechanism for the antioxidant and catalytic activities of CDs with evidence of effective electron and proton donating abilities. Overall, PPCCDs have the potential to be an effective antioxidant, a useful catalyst, and an efficient cell-imaging tool, acting as a nanomaterial for multifaceted applications.

Santanu Dhara: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
